questions.stem: This liver lesion is composed of dilated, thin-walled, blood-filled vascular channels lined by a single layer of flattened endothelium and separated by loose fibrous stroma. Which benign hepatic lesion does this histology represent?

explanation.summary: Hepatic hemangiomas are the most common benign liver tumors and consist of dilated, blood-filled vascular channels lined by endothelium with variably sized channels and loose fibrous stroma, reflecting their nature as congenital vascular malformations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatic hemangiomas are the most common benign liver tumors and consist of dilated, blood-filled vascular channels lined by endothelium with variably sized channels and loose fibrous stroma, reflecting their nature as congenital vascular malformations.

questions.stem: This medium-magnification histology shows sheets of tumor cells with abundant cytoplasm, large abnormal nuclei, and intracellular and extracellular brown bile pigment. Which malignancy is depicted?

explanation.summary: Hepatocellular carcinoma consists of sheets of high-grade tumor cells with abundant cytoplasm and large abnormal nuclei; the presence of bile production (brown pigment) is a distinguishing feature confirming hepatocellular origin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatocellular carcinoma consists of sheets of high-grade tumor cells with abundant cytoplasm and large abnormal nuclei; the presence of bile production (brown pigment) is a distinguishing feature confirming hepatocellular origin.

questions.stem: This CT shows a well-circumscribed hepatic lesion that enhances homogeneously and contains a central scar in an asymptomatic young adult with normal liver enzymes. Which lesion is most consistent with these findings?

explanation.summary: Focal nodular hyperplasia is a benign, non-hormone-dependent lesion characterized by hyperplastic hepatocytes arranged around a central fibrous (stellate) scar, appearing as a well-circumscribed homogeneously enhancing mass with a central scar on imaging.
explanation.whyCorrect: Focal nodular hyperplasia is a benign, non-hormone-dependent lesion characterized by hyperplastic hepatocytes arranged around a central fibrous (stellate) scar, appearing as a well-circumscribed homogeneously enhancing mass with a central scar on imaging.

questions.stem: This contrast-enhanced CT shows a solitary, well-demarcated hepatic mass that is bright during the arterial phase and lacks a central scar in a young woman with a history of oral contraceptive use. Which lesion is most likely?

explanation.summary: Hepatic adenomas are benign epithelial tumors strongly associated with oral contraceptive and anabolic steroid use; they are highly vascular and show bright arterial-phase enhancement without the central scar seen in focal nodular hyperplasia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatic adenomas are benign epithelial tumors strongly associated with oral contraceptive and anabolic steroid use; they are highly vascular and show bright arterial-phase enhancement without the central scar seen in focal nodular hyperplasia.

questions.stem: This CT (arrow) reveals a solitary hepatic mass in a patient with chronic hepatitis-related cirrhosis and an elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein. Which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?

explanation.summary: In a patient with cirrhosis, imaging evidence of a hepatic mass combined with an elevated serum AFP is usually sufficient to diagnose hepatocellular carcinoma without biopsy, which is contraindicated due to hemorrhage and seeding risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a patient with cirrhosis, imaging evidence of a hepatic mass combined with an elevated serum AFP is usually sufficient to diagnose hepatocellular carcinoma without biopsy, which is contraindicated due to hemorrhage and seeding risk.
